title: Proactive Agent Research Environment: Simulating Active Users to Evaluate Proactive Assistants
gist: Apple researchers (with UC Santa Barbara and University of Washington) introduce Pare, a framework that models apps as stateful finite state machines rather than flat tool-calling APIs to enable realistic simulation of active users, plus Pare-Bench, a 143-task benchmark testing whether proactive digital assistants can observe context, infer goals, time interventions, and orchestrate actions across multiple apps.

themes.theme: Realistic simulation as the missing piece for proactive agents

insights.claim: Existing frameworks for evaluating proactive digital assistants model apps as flat tool-calling APIs, which fails to capture the stateful, sequential nature of real user interaction and makes realistic simulation of active users infeasible.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific methodological gap holding back proactive-assistant development industry-wide (not just at Apple): benchmarks built on flat APIs can't test whether an agent correctly anticipates needs across a realistic, stateful app environment.
insights.quote: Existing approaches model apps as flat tool-calling APIs, failing to capture the stateful and sequential nature of user interaction in digital environments and making realistic user simulation infeasible.

insights.claim: Pare (Proactive Agent Research Environment) models applications as finite state machines with stateful navigation and a state-dependent action space, enabling an active user simulator that behaves more like a real person moving through an app.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A new simulation substrate that could become a standard testbed for evaluating proactive assistant products (e.g., a revamped Siri) before deployment, reducing real-world trial-and-error risk.
insights.quote: Pare models applications as finite state machines with stateful navigation and state-dependent action space for the user simulator, enabling active user simulation.

themes.theme: Benchmarking what proactive assistants must do

insights.claim: Pare-Bench is a benchmark of 143 diverse tasks spanning communication, productivity, scheduling, and lifestyle apps, designed specifically to test four proactive-agent capabilities: context observation, goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Defines the concrete capability checklist (observe, infer, time, orchestrate across apps) that Apple is using to measure progress toward a genuinely proactive assistant -- a leading indicator of what capabilities to watch for in future Apple AI product announcements.
insights.quote: we present Pare-Bench, a benchmark of 143 diverse tasks spanning communication, productivity, scheduling, and lifestyle apps, designed to test context observation, goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration

insights.claim: A related Apple paper on interactive digital agents (IDAs) notes that prior LLM-powered agent methods accomplish less than half of tasks in sophisticated benchmarks such as AppWorld, even though these agents can react to interface feedback across multi-step exchanges.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A sub-50% task-completion rate on a leading agent benchmark quantifies just how immature current LLM agents are at real app-interaction tasks, tempering near-term expectations for fully autonomous proactive assistants.
insights.quote: Prior methods accomplish less than half of tasks in sophisticated benchmarks such as AppWorld.
